items.prompt: A binary heap already contains h elements. Report the worst-case time complexity of reading the root with peek, without modifying the heap.

feedback.reason: Peek reads the root at a known position and performs no invariant repair.
feedback.details: The heap invariant guarantees that the selected extreme is stored at the root, so the operation needs one indexed read. The number of descendants does not matter because no traversal occurs. This constant bound applies to peek only; push and pop may follow a root-to-leaf or leaf-to-root path and therefore have different costs.

items.prompt: A binary heap contains h elements. Report the worst-case time complexity of one push or one pop that may repair the heap along one root-to-leaf path.

feedback.reason: A complete binary heap has height O(log h), and one mutation repairs at most one ancestor or descendant path.
feedback.details: Push may sift a new leaf to the root, and pop may sift a replacement root toward a leaf. Each level performs constant comparison and swap work, while the number of levels is logarithmic in the current heap size. The bound is not constant merely because the root is directly accessible, and it is not linear because unrelated branches are not scanned.

items.prompt: An algorithm processes n stream values and maintains at most k retained candidates in a binary heap. In the worst case, each value may cause one bounded heap update. Report total time and extra space.
constraints: Exclude storage for the input and final output.

feedback.reason: There are n inspections, each with at most logarithmic work in a heap whose size never exceeds k; the retained state stores at most k entries.
feedback.details: Every input value is examined once. A value may be discarded after a root comparison or may trigger push/pop repair bounded by the height of a size-k heap. The worst-case total is therefore O(n log k), with O(k) extra retained state. The bound is not O(n log n) unless the heap grows with n, and it is not O(n) in the worst case when k grows and many updates repair the heap.

items.prompt: All n values are already available. Compare bottom-up binary heap construction with inserting the n values one at a time into an initially empty heap.

feedback.reason: Bottom-up heapify has linear aggregate sift-down work, while n independent insertions can each climb logarithmic height.
feedback.details: In bottom-up heapify, most nodes are near the leaves and can move only a few levels; summing their possible work yields O(n), not n times the full height. Repeated insertion grows the heap incrementally, and the worst-case sum of logarithmic insertion heights is O(n log n). The methods produce the same heap contract but have different construction cost when all input is available in advance.

items.prompt: k sorted sources contain N values in total. A merge heap stores at most one current head per nonempty source. Report total time and extra space, excluding the output.

feedback.reason: Each of N outputs causes at most one pop and one push on a frontier bounded by k entries.
feedback.details: Every input value becomes an output exactly once. Selecting it and exposing its successor mutate a heap whose height is O(log k), so the total is O(N log k). The heap and source-position metadata use O(k) extra space. N counts iterations; k bounds per-iteration priority work. With two sources, direct merge gives O(N) time without a heap.

items.prompt: An algorithm performs n heap mutations. During the run, heap size varies but never exceeds H. What worst-case time bound follows directly from the heap-size cap?

feedback.reason: Each mutation costs at most O(log H), and there are n such mutations.
feedback.details: The precise sum depends on the size before each operation, but log hᵢ is at most log H. Summing n bounded mutation costs gives O(n log H). O(log H) omits the number of operations, while O(n) assumes H is constant or repairs never traverse height. This form transfers to bounded top-k heaps, merge frontiers, and schedulers with an explicit queue-size cap.
